coredata.dc:title: A bifunctional fluorescent gel for sensitive visual/fluorescent dual-mode detection and efficient adsorption of Pb2+ from wastewater

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yinhui
Mou, Mengshi
Dong, Lin
Yu, Hongmei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a fluorescent gel adsorbent (SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads) capable of achieving sensitive naked-eye/fluorescence dual-mode detection and efficient removal of Pb2+ was developed by integrating designed amino-modified carbon dots with CS2-modified sodium alginate gels. The adsorption properties of the fluorescent adsorbent were further optimized and analyzed using a series of response surface experiments. The adsorption isotherms fit well with the Langmuir model, and the adsorption kinetics fit with the pseudo-second-order model. The maximum adsorption concentration for Pb2+ was 607.08 mg/g, surpassing previously reported adsorbents. To obtain more accurate information on heavy metal concentrations than visual observation alone, we employed a smartphone-based color sensing application to assist in the detection process. In this mode, the color changes of the gel beads exhibited a reliable linear relationship with Pb2+ concentrations in the ranges of 0–50 mg/L and 60–100 mg/L, with R2 values of 0.958 and 0.978, respectively. Experimental results demonstrate that both detection modes (fluorescence and visual observation) reliably detect lead ions. This indicates that the gel has significant potential for monitoring lead pollution in real wastewater. This study establishes a multifunctional platform for the sensitive detection and efficient removal of Pb2+. The high value of adsorption capacity after five adsorption-desorption cycles indicated excellent reusability of the hydrogel absorbent. The biodegradability of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads was also evaluated.

head.ce:title: A bifunctional fluorescent gel for sensitive visual/fluorescent dual-mode detection and efficient adsorption of Pb2+ from wastewater

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Science and Technology Liaoning, Anshan, Liaoning 114000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology Liaoning

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of science and technology liaoning, Anshan, Liaoning, 114000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a fluorescent gel adsorbent (SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads) capable of achieving sensitive naked-eye/fluorescence dual-mode detection and efficient removal of Pb2+ was developed by integrating designed amino-modified carbon dots with CS2-modified sodium alginate gels. The adsorption properties of the fluorescent adsorbent were further optimized and analyzed using a series of response surface experiments. The adsorption isotherms fit well with the Langmuir model, and the adsorption kinetics fit with the pseudo-second-order model. The maximum adsorption concentration for Pb2+ was 607.08 mg/g, surpassing previously reported adsorbents. To obtain more accurate information on heavy metal concentrations than visual observation alone, we employed a smartphone-based color sensing application to assist in the detection process. In this mode, the color changes of the gel beads exhibited a reliable linear relationship with Pb2+ concentrations in the ranges of 0–50 mg/L and 60–100 mg/L, with R2 values of 0.958 and 0.978, respectively. Experimental results demonstrate that both detection modes (fluorescence and visual observation) reliably detect lead ions. This indicates that the gel has significant potential for monitoring lead pollution in real wastewater. This study establishes a multifunctional platform for the sensitive detection and efficient removal of Pb2+. The high value of adsorption capacity after five adsorption-desorption cycles indicated excellent reusability of the hydrogel absorbent. The biodegradability of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads was also evaluated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, environmental pollution by toxic heavy metals has become a global issue resulting in significant environmental concerns [1]. Lead is one of the most toxic elements to the human body, and is highly toxic even at ultra-trace levels [2]. Lead is ingested through food and lead dust, and is gradually absorbed and accumulated in the blood, bones, liver, kidneys, brain, and skin, posing a great threat to human health [3]. Thus, achieving the rapid detection [4] and efficient removal [5] of lead from environmental waters is of great importance. However, most existing materials are mostly capable of performing only a single detection or adsorption task, failing to meet the comprehensive requirements for heavy metal pollution control. In order to better prevent and control heavy metal pollution and to realize the integration of detection and removal of Pb2+, making it imperative to explore and develop versatile materials for Pb2+ detection and adsorption [6]. Currently, multifunctional materials including magnetic nanoparticles [7], metal/covalent organic frameworks (MOF/COF) [8,9], mesoporous nanocomposites [10,11], synthesized polymers [12] and hydrogels [13,14] have been developed for the simultaneous detection and adsorption of toxic metal ions. Among these materials, hydrogels have attracted special attention due to their flexible synthesis process, good hydrophilicity, three-dimensional porous structure and rich chelating groups [15].

Sodium alginate (SA) is a natural polysaccharide extracted from brown algae, which has the advantages of easy degradation, renewable and non-toxicity [16]. The high density of epoxy, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the SA gel not only improves the mechanical strength of the hydrogel, but also facilitates the adsorption of Pb2+ on the hydrogel. Notably, Sodium alginate hydrogel serves as a biocompatible carrier that maintains the original properties of the embedded material. This allows for the enhancement of the adsorption properties of the hydrogel material through the grafting of multiple functional group structures [17]. Liu et al. showed that CS and CS groups exhibit strong binding affinity for various metal ions, especially Pb2+ [18]. Incorporating these functional groups into adsorbent materials is expected to significantly enhance their adsorption capacity for Pb2+. Lian et al. used CS2 to prepare functionalized graphene oxide (GOCS) by introducing SC groups onto the surface of graphene oxide (GO) [19]. The study results showed that the functionalization with CS2 increased the maximum adsorption capacity of Pb2+ from 292.8 mg/g to 383.4 mg/g, representing a 31 % improvement over GO. Additionally, the equilibrium adsorption capacity increased by 83.2 %, from 152.97 mg/g to 280.2 mg/g.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are multifunctional nanomaterials with fluorescence stability, detection properties and adsorption properties [20], which are widely used in biomedicine [21], luminescent materials [22], environmental detection [23], food and agriculture [24]. Carbon quantum dots have many advantages, such as good water solubility, low toxicity, low cost and good biocompatibility [25]. By introducing carbon quantum dots into hydrogel polymers as grafting materials, the newly formed composite hydrogel combines the functionality of carbon quantum dots and hydrogel under the joint action of non-covalent and covalent bonds [26]. The optical [27], adsorption [28] and mechanical [29] properties of this composite gel material will be greatly improved. Therefore, the combination of CD and hydrogel is essentially a win-win strategy. On the one hand, the doping of CDs in hydrogels prevents fluorescence bursting (ACQ) caused by CDs due to aggregation, and its fluorescence stability is significantly improved. On the other hand, the CDs-doped composite hydrogel has stronger mechanical and adsorption properties [30,31].

In this study, a composite fluorescent gel material (SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads) that can realize the sensitive detection and efficient adsorption and removal of Pb2+ in wastewater by naked-eye/fluorescence dual-mode will be reported. SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads are based on sodium alginate gel as the matrix material. Calcium hydrogen phosphate (CaHPO4) is used as a porogenic agent to increase the gel's porosity. CS2 and CDs are added to create more active binding sites for lead ions and give the gel fluorescent properties. The adsorption behavior of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads on Pb2+ was systematically investigated by batch experiments, including the effects of the initial concentration of Pb2+, temperature and adsorption time on the adsorption amount and removal rate. The adsorption kinetic, adsorption isotherm, adsorption mechanism and detection mechanism were comprehensively investigated. The experimental results show that the method is suitable for the rapid detection and effective removal of low initial concentration of Pb2+ in real wastewater, and we have reason to believe that the reported SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads will provide a new solution for the development of multifunctional materials and the practical application of heavy metal pollution control.

Sodium alginate (SA, Na2CO3-H2O-C2H3O2, AR), Carbon disulfide (CS2, AR), Calcium hydrogen phosphate (CaHPO4, 99.9 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.5 %), calcium chloride (CaCl2, AR), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2, 99.9 %), Lead standard stock solution (1000 mg/L), and other reagents were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polyethyleneimine (PEI, 99 wt%, 99 % in water, Molecular Weight (Mw): 600) was purchased from Macklin Chemical (shanghai, China). The citric acid (CA, AR) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water.

To synthesize carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) with selective affinity for lead ions, 0.4 g of citric acid (CA) and 2.8 g of polyethyleneimine (PEI) were added to a 150 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene. Then, 120 mL of deionized water was added, and the mixture was ultrasonicated for 5 min to ensure uniformity. The reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 20 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 5 min) to obtain the supernatant. The supernatant was then dialyzed using a 500 Da dialysis bag for 36 h, with the deionized water being replaced every 8 h. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze-dried using a lyophilizer at −60 °C to obtain the powder. The N-CDs preparation was completed, and the resulting powder was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use. The synthesis process is shown in Scheme 1 .

The SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads were prepared using a simple one-pot method. First, 1 g of SA was added to 100 mL of 0.1 % sodium carbonate solution and stirred at 60 °C with a magnetic stirrer for 1 h to ensure the SA was evenly dispersed in the aqueous solution. After the solution was cooled to 40 °C, 5 ml of CS2 was added, and the mixture was stirred for an additional 8 h to allow thorough cross-linking. Next, 0.6 g of CaHPO4 was added, and stirring continued for another 4 h. The resulting solution was then uniformly dripped into a 1.5 % CaCl2 solution using a peristaltic pump, and the mixture was allowed to solidify for 8 h to form structurally stable, uniform spherical particles. The gel beads were rinsed with deionized water to remove any surface residues. The beads were then immersed in an HCl solution (0.1 mol/mL) until the CaHPO4 was completely decomposed, followed by rinsing with deionized water to remove any remaining residues. The beads were then placed into a 0.05 mg/L N-CDs solution and stirred with a magnetic stirrer for 24 h to ensure that the N-CDs were fully grafted onto the surface of the gel material. Any unreacted carbon dots on the surface of the beads were washed off with deionized water. Finally, the gel beads were freeze-dried at −50 °C using a lyophilizer to create the three-dimensional network structure of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads. The resulting gel beads had a diameter of approximately 2 mm. The preparation process of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads is shown in Scheme 2 .

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (NICOLET Is10, UK) recorded the infrared spectra of N-CDs and gels. The fluorescence properties of fluorescent SA/CS2/N-CQDs/gel beads were measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer (FS5, Germany). The surface morphology of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads and the dispersion and size of the carbon dots were observed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEM-F200, Japan). The chemical compositions of the N-CDs and adsorbed fluorescent gels were studied by XPS (ESCALABXi+, UK). The freeze-dried gel was sprayed with gold, and the surface morphology of the sample was observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SIGMA500, Germany). The surface morphology of the sample was examined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) with point scanning, and its elemental composition was analyzed using EDS. Gel beads were prepared by a vertical freeze-drying machine (YTLG-12A, China). The concentrations of different metal ions were determined by inductively coupled atomic emission spectrometer (ICP, PQ9000, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of N-CDs and SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads

ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images of the N-CDs and SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads are shown in Fig. 1 . As observed from the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images in Fig. 1(a), the N-CDs appear as uniformly spherical particles. A statistical analysis of the particle sizes in the TEM images estimates that the average diameter of the synthesized N-CDs is approximately 1.78 nm, with good dispersion and no significant aggregation. Additionally, Fig. 3(c) shows that particles with the same structure as the N-CDs are uniformly distributed on the gel surface, indicating that the N-CDs were successfully grafted onto the surface of the gel beads.

The macroscopic images, SEM images, and EDS analysis results of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads before (a) and after (b) the adsorption of Pb2+ are shown in Fig. 2 . The macroscopic images show that the fluorescent color of the gel beads changes significantly after adsorption, while the overall structure of the gel beads remains stable. This indicates that the composite gel has a good response to lead ions and maintains stability. The SEM images reveal that the gel beads exhibit a three-dimensional porous structure before adsorption, with abundant internal pores. After the adsorption of lead ions, the pore structure of the gel beads becomes more compact, which is likely due to ion exchange between Pb2+ and some of the Ca2+ during the adsorption process [2,32]. Furthermore, irregular wrinkled particle structures appear on the surface of the gel beads after adsorption, mainly due to the formation of chelates between Pb2+ and the functional groups on the gel surface [16]. These findings are consistent with the conclusions drawn from XPS characterization. The EDS analysis shows the appearance of a lead peak in the distribution map after adsorption, confirming the successful adsorption of Pb2+ onto the gel beads. Additionally, the elemental scan shows that not only does Pb2+ appear on the gel beads after adsorption, and the distribution density of Ca decreases, further demonstrating that Pb2+ was successful adsorbed onto the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads [33]. The reduced density of Ca2+ and the decreased porosity of the gel beads after adsorption suggest that the adsorption process involved a cation exchange mechanism [34].

Fig. 3 presented the IR spectra of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs), SA/CS2 gel beads, and the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads. For the case of N-CDs (Fig. 3(c)), the stretching vibrations of the OH, CO, CN and CO groups were presented at 3420 [35], 1280 [36] 1660 and 1130 cm−1 [37], respectively. This indicates that there may be a large amount of OH, COOH and NH2 on the surface of N-CDs. In the FTIR spectrum of the SA/CS2 gel beads (Fig. 3(b)). The adsorption bands at 820 cm−1, and 880 cm−1 in SA/CS2 gel beads were identified as coming from the CS and CS stretching vibrations [38], respectively. For the case of the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads (Fig. 3(a)), characteristic peaks for CS, CS, CO, CN, and CO appear at 820 cm−1, 940 cm−1, 1040 cm−1, 1300 cm−1, and 1660 cm−1, respectively. The absorption peak at 3420 cm−1 is stronger in the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads compared to N-CDs and the SA/CS2 gel beads [39,40]. These observations may be due to the physical doping between CS2, N-CDs and SA due to the hydrogen bonds between them (CO/N⋯OOH) and the coordination in the interaction between SA and Ca2+ [41]. This indicates that N-CDs and CS2, serving as the nitrogen and sulfur sources, were successfully incorporated into the SA/CS2 gel beads, resulting in the successful synthesis of the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads.

To elucidate the adsorption mechanism, XPS characterization was performed on the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads before and after Pb2+ adsorption. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the presence of new peaks corresponding to Pb 4f in the XPS spectra confirms that Pb2+ has been adsorbed onto the hydrogel, and it can be observed that the content of Ca2+ decreases after adsorption [42,43]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Pb 4f (Fig. 4(b)) shows a peak at 138.72 eV, which is characteristic of divalent metal ions (Pb2+), while the peak at 137.98 eV corresponds to the metallic complex center Pb0. This suggests that Pb2+ is primary adsorbed onto the hydrogel surface through the formation of stable multi-ligand chelates involving two or more functional groups present on the surface [44]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 5(a)) indicates that the binding energies of NH and CN in the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads show no significant change before and after the adsorption of Pb2+. This result suggests that while NH2/NR groups on the hydrogel do participate in the adsorption of Pb2+, their capture capability is relatively weak. The comparison of high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s (Fig. 5(b)) shows varying degrees of binding energy shifts associated with OH, CO, and CO, which can be attributed to electrostatic interactions between COO− and Pb2+, as well as coordination between oxygen atoms and Pb2+ [45]. As depicted in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 5(c)), the binding energies of CS and CS in the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads before adsorption are 161.58 eV and 162.63 eV [46], respectively. After adsorption, the binding energies of CS and CS increase to 161.68 eV and 162.98 eV, respectively, with a significant change in peak intensity before and after adsorption. This indicates that functional groups containing these two bonds play a strong role in the adsorption of Pb2+ [18]. In summary, NH2/NR, OH, COOH, and CS/CS are the primary adsorption groups in the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads material, and ions exchange also plays a role in the adsorption process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence characterization of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image (Fig. 6(a)) indicates that the N-CDs grafted onto the surface of the gel beads appear as granular structures, with an average diameter of 1.78 nm. Fig. 6(b) shows that within the range of 320 nm to 410 nm, the position of the fluorescence emission peak remains unchanged (around 442 nm) as the excitation wavelength increases. This indicates that the fluorescence emission of the gel beads is not dependent on the excitation wavelength [47]. The excitation-independent emission behavior is highly related to the uniform surface state of the gel beads, suggesting that the carbon quantum dots are uniformly distributed on the gel surface. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the hydrogel (Fig. 6(c)) displays three absorption peaks at 207 nm, 245 nm, and 370 nm. The peaks at 207 nm and 245 nm are attributed to π-π* electronic transitions of CC bonds [48], while the peak at 370 nm can be explained by n-π* electronic transitions of CO/CN groups [49]. When the excitation wavelength (EX) is 350 nm, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength is 442 nm. As shown in Fig. 6(d), the addition of lead ions (Pb2+) resulted in an increase in the fluorescence lifetime of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from 4.70 ns to 7.18 ns [50]. This suggests that Pb2+ may form stable complexes with the functional groups on the surface of the CQDs, thereby reducing non-radiative decay pathways and leading to an increase in fluorescence lifetime [51].

To investigate the fluorescence stability of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads at different pH values, the fluorescence intensity of the gel was tested across a pH range of 2 to 9 under the same conditions, as shown in Fig. 6(f). It was found that the fluorescence was strongest at pH 3, with only a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity at pH 2, 4, and 5. This indicates that the composite gel exhibits good fluorescence stability within the pH range of 2–5. Since Pb2+ forms precipitates at pH > 5.5, this experimental result aligns well with the pH conditions for lead ion adsorption.

To study the ion selectivity of the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads, ten ions potentially present in lead wastewater were randomly selected: Na+, K+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Cl−, and NO3 −. The test results from Fig. 6(e) show that the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads have a specific selectivity for Pb2+, with minimal interference from other common ions in water. The fluorescence of the gel significantly enhances upon the addition of Pb2+, which is likely due to the presence of NH2, OH, and COOH groups on the N-CDs surface. Pb2+ forms a cooperative chelation reaction with NH2, resulting in the formation of an N-CDs/Pb2+ complex [52]. This complex hinders the photo-induced electron transfer process, leading to fluorescence enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence intensity sensitivity and detection limit determination

ce:section.ce:para: As the concentration of Pb2+ increases, the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogel gradually increases. A linear fit using the Stern-Volmer equation was performed, with F1/F0 as the ordinate and Pb2+ concentration as the abscissa [53]. In the concentration range of 0–50 μmol/L, F1/F0 exhibits a good linear relationship with Pb2+ concentration, with an R2 value of 0.993 (Fig. 7(b)). To estimate the specific detection limit, the detection limit (LOD) was calculated using the equation LOD = 3σ/s, where “σ” is the standard deviation of 10 repeated measurements of the blank sample. “s” is the slope of the linear relationship curve between (I - I0) and the amount of metal ion adsorbed [54]. The estimated detection limit of the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads for Pb2+ ions was 0.12 μmol/L. This detection limit meets the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's requirements for lead in drinking water, indicating that this hydrogel can be used to monitor actual Pb2+ contamination in water bodies under fluorescence mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual inspection based on smartphones with the naked eye
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 7(c), the appearance of the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads does not change under sunlight after adsorbing Pb2+. However, under a 350 nm excitation light source, the fluorescence color of the gel beads shows a clear and regular gradient change after exposure to different concentrations of lead solutions, with the fluorescence color gradually shifting from dark blue to bright indigo. Thus, within the detection range, the presence of Pb2+ in the environment can be determined by visually observing the color change of the substrate. To obtain more accurate information on heavy metal concentrations without the need for expensive instruments such as ICP. We designed a smartphone application to assist in the detection process [55]. By using the smartphone as a signal recognizer and analyzer, we successfully established a portable fluorescence sensing platform for the rapid on-site detection of Pb2+ in real water samples. The steps for detecting heavy metal Pb2+ using the smartphone application through a colorimetric reaction are as follows:
1)
Prepare a Pb2+ concentration gradient of 0, 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 80, and 100 mg/L. Add the hydrogel bead material to each of these solutions. After the reaction is complete, use a smartphone to take fluorescence photos of the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads in each of the different Pb2+ concentration solutions.
2)
Use the “Color Recognition” app on the smartphone to identify the color signal in the photos and convert it into RGB values [56]. The green channel (G) and blue channel (B) values are used to calculate the RGB ratio. The working curve is established by plotting the G/B ratio (Y) against Pb2+ concentration (X). As shown in Fig. 7(d), in the ranges of 0–50 mg/L and 60–100 mg/L, the color change exhibits a reliable linear relationship with Pb2+ concentration, with R2 values of 0.958 and 0.978, respectively. This indicates that the hydrogel material has a sensitive fluorescence color response to lead ions.
3)
Take a photo of the hydrogel that has fully reacted with the sample to be tested to obtain the usable color of the hydrogel material. Based on the color data of the standard and test samples, the approximate concentration of the sample can be calculated by comparing the color value of the test sample to its position on the standard curve.

This smartphone-based detection method for Pb2+ heavy metal ions in water samples allows non-professionals to handle the color reaction results of the hydrogel material with water samples. Through color recognition and analysis, this method enables semi-quantitative detection of Pb2+ concentrations in aqueous solution [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of N-CDs is crucial for achieving fluorescence-based detection of Pb2+ using the hydrogel. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) rich in amino groups were prepared via a hydrothermal method, and previous FTIR and XPS characterizations have confirmed the successful synthesis of N-CDs. The N-CDs possess a large number of functional groups (NH2, COOH, OH) that can chelate with Pb2+ ions. These functional groups not only provide additional binding sites for the adsorption of lead ions but are also key to the specific recognition of lead by the composite hydrogel [28,30]. Thus, exploring the fluorescence enhancement recognition mechanism of N-CDs for Pb2+ can effectively explain the detection mechanism of Pb2+ by the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads. In Fig. 8 , the TEM image shows that the N-CDs are uniform distributed on the surface of the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads. When the hydrogel material comes into contact with Pb2+ in an aqueous solution, the functional group structures on the hydrogel surface rapidly capture the lead ions [58], facilitating a synergistic chelation reaction between the NH, OH, and COOH groups in the N-CDs and Pb2+. This results in the formation of an N-CDs/Pb2+ complex [51], which hinders the process of photo induced electron transfer, thereby leading to fluorescence enhancement [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of Pb2+on SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads

ce:section.ce:para: The initial pH of the solution is a crucial parameter that influences the adsorption performance and capacity of the adsorbent. pH affects the protonation or deprotonation of functional groups on the surface of the hydrogel adsorbent, which in turn influences the adsorbent's ability to capture metal ions from the aqueous solution [60]. To determine the optimal pH for removing lead ions through adsorption, a series of Pb2+ adsorption experiments were conducted at an initial concentration of 50 mg/L by varying the pH value. As shown in Fig. 9(a), the optimal pH is 4, at which the maximum adsorption capacity of the SA/CS2/N-CDs hydrogel for Pb2+ reaches 204.19 mg/g. Previous studies have reported that the significant impact of pH on Pb2+ adsorption performance is due to the effect of surface charge on the adsorbent. Fig. 9(b) shows that the pHpzc (point of zero charge) of the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads s is 3.95. The surface charge of the hydrogel becomes increasingly negative as the pH increases from 1.0 to 5.0, which enhances the adsorption and removal of Pb2+ by the gel beads. Under conditions where pH > pHpzc, the negatively charged binding sites on the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads facilitate the capture of lead ions from the aqueous solution through electrostatic attraction, thereby promoting the adsorption capacity for Pb2+ [61]. The optimal pH value for the adsorption performance of the gel beads was determined to be 4.

To investigate the effect of coexisting interfering ions on the adsorption performance of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads, solutions containing 50 mg/L of cations such as Na+, K+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, and Al3+ were prepared [62]. The impact of these metal ions on the adsorption of Pb2+ by the gel beads was studied under conditions where Pb2+ was present with the aforementioned metal ions. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 9(c). In the presence of coexisting ions, the adsorption capacity of the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads for Pb2+ decreased to varying extents depending on the type of metal ions added. Monovalent cations (Na+ and K+) had minimal impact on the adsorption performance of the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads. The effect of divalent cations (Zn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, and Mg2+) on the adsorption capacity was also limited. This limited impact may be attributed to the strong ion-dipole interactions between Na+, K+, Zn2+, Cu2+, and Mg2+ and water molecules, which hinder the electrostatic attraction between these metal ions and the functional groups on the surface of the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads [63]. Consequently, Pb2+ can form stable metal complexes with the lone pair electrons on the gel beads through electron sharing, leading to minimal interference from these ions. In contrast, trivalent cations (Cr3+ and Al3+) significantly affected the adsorption of Pb2+. This is due to the competition between Pb2+ and the trivalent cations for the negatively charged adsorption sites on the hydrogel surface in a low pH environment. Despite this competition, the decrease in the adsorption capacity for Pb2+ was only slight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial concentration and contact time
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9(d) demonstrates the effects of initial concentration and contact time on the adsorption of Pb2+ by SA gel beads and SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads. As illustrated, the initial concentrations of Pb2+ were 20, 50, and 100 mg/L. The equilibrium adsorption capacity of Pb2+ by both SA gel beads and SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads increased significantly with increasing initial concentrations. Specifically, as the concentration increased from 20 to 50 mg/L, the equilibrium adsorption capacity of the SA gel beads and SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads increased from 66.22 mg/g and 73.77 mg/g to 292.79 mg/g and 336.42 mg/g, respectively. This trend is likely due to the higher initial concentration providing a stronger driving force, facilitating the complexation of Pb2+ with the functional groups present on the surface of the SA gel beads and SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads [64]. Moreover, when the Pb2+ concentration was below 50 mg/L, the adsorption efficiency of the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads for Pb2+ approached 100 %, indicating their superior adsorption performance at lower Pb2+ concentrations.

On the other hand, during the initial 0–30 min of adsorption, both the SA gel beads and SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads exhibited a rapid increase in adsorption capacity, attributable to the abundance of active sites on the hydrogels [65]. After 30 min, the rate of increase in adsorption capacity slowed, eventually reaching equilibrium. Additionally, as indicated in the Fig. 9(d), the adsorption equilibrium time for the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads was 30 min, which is significantly shorter than the 60 min required for the SA gel beads. This demonstrates that the incorporation of CS2 and N-CDs significantly enhanced the adsorbent's performance, reducing the time needed to reach adsorption equilibrium.

Fig. 9(e) illustrates the adsorption capacity of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads in both ideal and real water conditions. The experimental results indicate that the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads exhibit excellent adsorption and removal efficiency for lead ions across different water qualities. When the initial concentration of Pb2+ is 50 mg/L, the residual Pb2+ concentration in the solution after adsorption is <1.0 mg/L, meeting the industry's residual limits. This experimental outcome demonstrates that the composite hydrogel possesses good stability and reliability for the removal of lead ions in real water samples, suggesting its significant application potential in the treatment of industrial wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and regeneration experiment of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 9(f), after undergoing five adsorption-desorption cycles, the removal efficiency of Pb2+ by the gel decreased from 98.3 % to 91.6 %. This reduction is main attributed to the fact that hydrochloric acid neutralizes a small number of negatively charged groups and displaces some Ca2+ [15,66], which affects the pore structure of the gel. Consequently, the adsorption capacity of the gel beads decreases slightly after each cycle. However, even after multiple “adsorption-desorption” cycles, the removal efficiency remains above 90 %, indicating that the gel material exhibits good reversibility and reusability in the adsorption of Pb2+ from aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodegradation experiment of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 10 , after 30 days, the gel in the PBS solution containing trypsin was almost completely degraded, whereas the gel in the PBS solution without trypsin remained unchanged [14]. This indicates that the gel possesses good biodegradability. Compared to traditional adsorbents and chemical precipitation methods, this adsorbent can be rapidly degraded after wastewater treatment, significantly reducing the risk of secondary pollution.

where qe denotes the equilibrium adsorption capacity of Pb2+ (mg/g); qt (mg/L) is the adsorption capacity of Pb2+ at time t. K2 is the rate constant of quasi-second-order adsorption; α is the initial sorption rate (mg/g), and β is the desorption constant (g/mg).

As shown in Fig. 11(a), the amount of lead ion adsorption by the gel beads increases sharply at first and then gradually levels off with increasing adsorption time. The adsorption of Pb2+ by the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads reached equilibrium within 60 min, indicating high adsorption efficiency. The equilibrium adsorption capacity was calculated to be 207.62 mg/g. During the adsorption process, the SA/CS2/N-CDs SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads initially chelate or coordinate with the metal ions, utilizing surface functional groups such as CS/CS, NH2, COOH, and OH [7]. Additionally, a small amount of Pb2+ can penetrate into the interior of the gel beads through swelling, where it is captured by internal functional groups via complexation. The fitting results of the adsorption data are shown in Table 1 . Upon comparing the R2 values of the three kinetic models, it is evident that the pseudo-second-order kinetic model, with an R2 value of 0.963, fits the data better than the other two models. Furthermore, the theoretical equilibrium adsorption capacity calculated using the pseudo-second-order kinetics model was 211.62 mg/g, which closely matches the experimentally obtained adsorption capacity. Therefore, it is confirmed that the adsorption behavior of the gel beads aligns more closely with the pseudo-second-order kinetics, suggesting that the adsorption behavior is primarily governed by chemical adsorption through coordination or chelation of Pb2+ with SH, NH2, COOH, and OH groups [67].

where Qmax is the maximum adsorption capacity of SA/H+ gel beads (mg/g), K is the constant for Langmuir isotherm, Kf is the constant for Freundlich isotherm; and 1/n is the surface inhomogeneity constant or the strength of adsorption.

To further explore the adsorption behavior of the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads, the experimental results were fitted using the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models [68]. The fitting results are shown in Fig. 11(b), and the relevant parameters are listed in Table 2 . As depicted in Fig. 11(b), the adsorption capacity gradually increases with the increase in the initial concentration of the solution, reaching a maximum of 607.08 mg/g. This is primarily due to the higher concentration increasing the likelihood of Pb2+ coming into contact with the adsorption sites, thereby enhancing the adsorption of Pb2+. By comparing the R2 values of the two models in Table 2, it is evident that the Langmuir isotherm model better describes the adsorption behavior of Pb2+ onto the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads. According to the Langmuir model fitting results, the theoretical maximum adsorption capacity of the SA/CS2-N-CDs gel beads for lead ions is 715.8 mg/g, which is close to the experimentally measured value. This indicates that the Langmuir model more accurately explains the adsorption process of Pb2+ by the composite hydrogel material, suggesting that the adsorption process is predominantly surface adsorption, characterized by monolayer adsorption [69].

Three thermodynamics properties, Gibbs Free Energy of Adsorption (△G°), the Enthalpy change (△H°), and the Entropy change (△S°) can provide more information regarding the thermodynamics during the adsorption process. The linear form of Van't Hoff was utilized to calculate the △G°, △H° and △S° based on the equations listed below.

As shown in Fig. 11(c), when the solution pH is adjusted to 4 and the initial concentration of Pb2+ ranges from 10 to 300 mg/L, the adsorption capacity of the hydrogel for lead ions increases continuously with rising temperature (288 K to 318 K). This clearly indicates that increasing the adsorption temperature is favorable for the process.

As shown in Fig. 11(d), at temperatures of 288 K, 298 K, 308 K, and 318 K, the change in Gibbs free energy (ΔG°) is negative, indicating that the adsorption of Pb2+ by the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads occurs spontaneously [70]. In addition, aΔG° gradually becomes smaller with increasing temperature, indicating that increasing temperature is more favorable for the adsorption of lead ions by the adsorbent，as shown in Table 3 . On the other hand, the ΔH° = 35.73 kJ/mol of SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads indicated that the adsorption of lead ions by the adsorbent was a heat-absorbing process. It aligns with the findings from the “effect of temperature” experiment in the adsorption batch test. The adsorption of ΔS° of Pb2+ on SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads was positive, which indicated that both degrees of freedom and entropy of the adsorption system were increasing during the adsorption process. It means that the chance of collision of lead ions with the surface of the adsorbent increases with the increase of temperature, thus promoting the adsorbent to capture the lead ions in the aqueous solution [71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption mechanism of SA/CS2-N-CDs gel beads
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the adsorption mechanism of Pb2+ by SA/CS2-N-CDs gel beads, SEM was used to analyze the surface structure of the gel beads, and FT-IR was employed to examine the chemical composition of the gel beads before and after Pb2+ adsorption [72]. As shown in Fig. 12(a), the peak at 3420 cm−1 in the spectrum before adsorption is attributed to the stretching vibration of OH. The strong peak at 1600 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO in the COOH group. The peak at 1300 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of CN, while the peaks at 940 cm−1 and 880 cm−1 are associated with the vibrations of CS and CS, respectively. It is evident that the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads exhibit characteristic peaks of functional groups such as OH, COOH, NH2, and CS/CS. After the adsorption of Pb2+, these characteristic peaks shift or decrease in intensity. For example, the peak at 1600 cm−1 shifts to 1660 cm−1, and the peaks at 1300 cm−1, 940 cm−1, and 880 cm−1 significantly decrease in intensity. These results indicate that the functional groups OH, COOH, NH2, and CS/CS are all involved in the adsorption process of Pb2+ [73,74]. These experimental results are consistent with the XPS analysis. In previous studies, EDS analysis showed a decrease in the density of Ca2+ after the adsorption of lead ions, confirming that ion exchange also plays a role in the adsorption process. Additionally, pH optimization experiments revealed that the pH of the solution decreased after Pb2+ adsorption, indicating that H+ participated in the exchange reaction. Furthermore, the surface structure of the gel beads is shown in Fig. 12(b). As depicted, the surface of the gel beads exhibits an irregular porous structure, which significantly increases their specific surface area and facilitates the capture of Pb2+ from water. The adsorption mechanism of SA/CS2-N-CDs gel beads is illustrated in Fig. 12(c).

In this study, a hydrogel composite adsorbent material (SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads) with real-time visual response was prepared by porogenic and chemical cross-linking methods. The material as an adsorbent is a three-dimensional mesh structure with extremely high porosity, which provides more active sites for adsorption of metal ions. Meanwhile, the uniformly distributed CDs on the surface of the hydrogel and the structure of functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and sulfhydryl groups abundant on the surface enhanced the aggregation and chelating effect on lead ions, which made the hydrogel material have dual functions of adsorption and detection. The experimental results showed that the removal rate of Pb2+ by the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads was close to 100 % when the initial lead ion concentration was 50 mg/L, and the limiting adsorption amount could reach 607.08 mg/g. The detection experiments showed that the SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads had satisfactory results in both fluorescence and smartphone-assisted naked-eye dual modes for the detection of Pb2+ in the aqueous solution. Under the fluorescence detection mode, the concentration of Pb2+ showed a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.993) in the range of 0–50 μmol/L, and the detection limit was 0.12 μmol/L. Under the smartphone-assisted condition, the color of the gel spheres showed a reliable linear relationship with the concentration of lead ions in the ranges of 0–50 and 60–100 mg/L, with R2 values were 0.958 and 0.978, respectively. In summary, the prepared fluorescent hydrogel adsorbents showed reliable adsorption and detection of lead ions in wastewater. The SA/CS2/N-CDs gel beads as an emerging force in the field of biobased adsorbent materials, which will promote the development of green bifunctional adsorbents.
